data.title: Wipro Has More Growth Challenges As Transition To GenAI Platform Continues
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Wipro Limited (WIT) reported FQ3 2026 financial results, missing revenue but meeting EPS consensus estimates.

I previously analyzed WIT in June 2025 with a Sell outlook due to declining revenue and client ramp-downs.

Forward revenue is still expected to decline, and clients remain focused on less lucrative cost-takeout work rather than high-value-added transformational projects.

While management is focused on shifting the firm’s focus toward GenAI capabilities and offerings, soft client demand keeps me pessimistic on the company's prospects in the near term, so I remain a Sell on WIT.

Wipro’s Market And Approach

Wipro generates its revenue from a combination of consulting, IT services and business process outsourcing income across a number of verticals and global regions.

The pie chart below shows the company's revenue contribution by region, with the Americas representing the strong majority of revenue:

The firm also generates a high percentage of revenue by industry from the BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance) vertical.

WIT’s main service offering categories are:

  • Consulting

  • IT services

  • Business process outsourcing

  • Government services

  • Product engineering & design

  • Other.

The 2024 global market for software consulting was around $350 billion in size and was expected to surpass $1.1 trillion by 2034, per a market research report by Precedence Research.

If achieved, this growth rate would equal a CAGR of 12.6% from 2025 to 2034, which is a fairly strong growth rate for an already large industry.

The main drivers for this forecasted growth are continued demand by organizations for efficiency improvements to their IT systems; also, the SMB market is expected to grow at the highest growth rate through 2034.

The North America region accounted for the highest market share in 2023, followed by Europe and the Asia Pacific region, as the chart shows here:

Substantial competitors and other market players include:

  • Capgemeni (CAPMF)

  • Accenture (ACN)

  • Atos SE (AEXAF)

  • CGI Group (GIB)

  • Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu

  • Cognizant (CTSH)

  • Infosys (INFY)

  • Ernst & Young

  • IBM (IBM)

  • PricewaterhouseCoopers

  • SAP SE (SAP)

  • Rapport IT

  • Oracle Corp. (ORCL)

  • Others.

Recent industry trends for consulting have been to ramp up generative AI consulting and implementation capabilities to meet client demand, at least for initial pilot projects in cost-takeout engagement areas.

Cloud modernization has also been a significant growth driver, especially in the BFSI, health and TMT sectors, while consumer and the EMR sectors have been softer in recent periods.

Recent Financial Trends

Topline revenue has resumed sequential growth, with four out of five industry sectors improving quarter-over-quarter.

The company's core operating leverage improved sequentially as operating margin improved on higher revenue against stable operating expenses.

Gross profit (blue line) dipped sequentially and year-over-year, while SG&A rose on the last of the company's restructuring costs and new labor code cost compliance.

Earnings per share dipped to $0.03, which has been the trend for much of the firm’s recent history, 2H 2024 and 1H 2025 notwithstanding.

A table showing various trailing twelve-month and forward performance metrics and estimates is shown here:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

1.7

EV/EBITDA - FWD

8.8

Rev. Growth Rate Est. - FWD

-0.6%

FY 2026 FWD EPS Estimate

$0.14

Price/Sales - TTM

2.1

P/E GAAP - TTM

14.32

Revenue Growth Rate - TTM

2.4%

Net Income Margin - TTM

14.6%

EBITDA Margin - TTM

18.5%

Market Capitalization

$21,070,000,000

Enterprise Value

$17,060,000,000

Operating Cash Flow

$1,730,000,000

EPS (Fully Diluted)

$0.14

Free Cash Flow per Share - TTM

$0.15

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.64

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

Capital Allocation

WIT ended FQ3 2026 with $6.4 billion in cash, equivalents and short-term or trading asset securities and $1.8 billion in total debt, virtually all of which was due within twelve months.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve-month period ending December 31, 2025, was approximately $1.5 billion after $196.5 million in total capital expenditures for the period.

So, capex was 1.9% of trailing twelve-month revenue, an asset-light figure typical of consulting firms at scale.

Stock-based compensation [SBC] was just $47.4 million for the period, or 0.5% of revenue, a low figure given the firm’s revenue scale.

In January 2026, the company declared an interim dividend of 6 Indian Rupees per share, and that prior regulatory impediments to stock repurchases were no longer a factor, leaving buybacks an ‘option on the table’.

Overall, Wipro appears to be a disciplined capital allocator with low SBC, low capex intensity and high capacity for dividends or buybacks.

Why I’m Negative On Wipro

Wipro is extensively aligning itself around an AI-first approach to its capabilities under what it calls Wipro Intelligence.

The company is developing industry-focused AI-enhanced platforms and networks for healthcare, lending and automotive sectors.

It is also moving forward on internal & delivery systems upgrades for operations, infrastructure support, BPO and application management.

The point is to move up the value-add stack from labor-centric services toward repeatable intellectual property and platform offerings.

This direction appears to highlight a future where the company may have significantly fewer human employees and a far greater number of GenAI agents and functionalities.

But, while clients have been interested in pilot programs in cost-takeout areas like customer service, finance and procurement, the number of successful projects is a fraction of the total number of engagements, as customers deal with a steep learning curve for integrating probabilistic GenAI technologies in functions that require deterministic output.

Furthermore, GenAI technologies themselves present some disruption risk to lower-value services such as BPO, especially for simpler business functions like initial customer service contact and triage.

The graphic shown here illustrates the frequency of various negative-oriented keywords in the most recent conference call between management and analysts:

The chart shows that Wipro’s clients continue to face macroeconomic uncertainties related to tariffs and expected headwinds in demand for FQ4 2026.

Looking ahead, management guided revenue growth to between 0% to 2% growth for FQ4 2026, not exactly a ringing growth trajectory. The company does not provide guidance on margins.

As to valuation, I’ve compared Wipro to competitor Infosys (INFY), with the table below showing various major metrics:

Metric

Infosys

Wipro Limited

Variance

P/E GAAP - TTM

17.4

14.32

-17.7%

EV/Sales - FWD

2.8

1.7

-37.5%

EV/EBITDA - FWD

11.7

8.8

-24.8%

Rev. Growth Rate Est. - FWD

3.8%

-0.6%

-116.1%

Net Income Margin - TTM

16.2%

14.6%

-9.7%

FCF Yield Per Share - TTM

7.03%

7.01%

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

Infosys is clearly being valued by the market at higher multiples on expected forward topline revenue growth and higher net margin than Wipro, and deservedly so, in my opinion.

While Wipro is transitioning its focus toward a GenAI-centric future, so are other consulting firms.

Customers are still more focused on less-lucrative cost-takeout work rather than higher value-add transformational engagements, so consulting firms have been stuck with keeping their employee headcount high despite a less valuable demand environment from clients.

A potentially favorable aspect for the stock is the ability of management to begin stock repurchases, although management is just hinting at having that on the table for consideration.

So, that could be a forward catalyst or at least a support for the stock, but the timing and amount are unknown, if it occurs at all.

With $4.8 billion in net cash, investors will need to wait and see.

However, in the meantime, the company is producing an estimated forward negative topline revenue growth, while clients dither and delay transformational engagements in favor of cost reduction work.

This is a common challenge for the entire consulting industry, not just Wipro, and the issues may not be resolved for some time, despite the improving capabilities of GenAI models.

So, for investors who think the company will embark on a major stock buyback effort, the stock may be a Hold, but I’m more skeptical and remain a Sell on WIT due to revenue contraction, continued GenAI doldrums and a likely bloated employee headcount that will need reducing for the firm to fully embrace the AI-centric future it claims to be moving towards.

data.title: U.K. February Inflation: Stable Headline Rate Masks Rising Retail And Housing Costs, GBP/USD Steady
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Data from the ONS showed the UK's annual inflation rate held firm at 3% in February 2026, matching the previous month's figure and meeting market expectations. This consistency marks a continued period of relative stability, with inflation remaining at its lowest point since March 2025. While the headline figure remained unchanged, the underlying data revealed shifting price pressures across various sectors.

Primary Drivers of Price Growth

The most significant upward pressure came from the clothing and footwear sector, which saw prices climb by 0.9%. This represents the first increase in four months, largely driven by the seasonal arrival of new spring collections following the conclusion of January sales. Additionally, costs for housing and utilities experienced a slight acceleration, rising to 4.6% from 4.5% in January.

Sectors Seeing a Slowdown

Conversely, several categories helped keep the headline rate in check:

  • Transport: Prices slowed to 2.4% (down from 2.7%), primarily due to a drop in motor fuel costs. Petrol prices fell by 1.6 pence per litre this month, a sharp contrast to the 2.0 pence per litre increase seen during the same period last year.
  • Essential Goods and Leisure: Food inflation eased to 3.3%, while recreation and culture slowed slightly to 2.5%.
  • Hospitality and Services: Costs for restaurants and hotels cooled to 4%, and the closely watched services inflation rate ticked down to 4.3%.

Overall, the data suggests a balancing act where rising retail and housing costs are being offset by cheaper fuel and a gradual cooling in service and food prices.

Inflation expectations soar on Iran war fears

The Bank of England (BoE) faces an increasingly complex policy environment as new data released on Tuesday revealed a surge in public inflation expectations. This shift in sentiment compounds an already difficult situation for policymakers, as manufacturers have reported their sharpest cost increases since 1992, pressures that are expected to be passed on to consumers in the near future.

Household energy tariffs are currently capped, a scheduled price adjustment in July looms as a significant upcoming catalyst for further inflation. These mounting pressures have created a notable divide between market participants and economic forecasters regarding the BoE's next move.

As of Tuesday, per LSEG data, investors were pricing in nearly three quarter-point interest rate hikes before the end of the year to combat rising prices. This should keep GBP partially supported in the interim.

However, any significant escalation to tensions in the Middle East could see the US dollar surge once more, and this could drag on cable.

The initial market reaction

Markets seemed to shrug off today's data, with GBP/USD remaining largely flat after the release.

Looking at the bigger picture technical outlook, GBP/USD is caught between long-term bearish momentum and a recent short-term recovery.

The pair is currently trading in a "squeeze" between two critical Simple Moving Averages (SMAs). While the price has recovered from its mid-March lows, it remains capped by the 200-period SMA (dark blue) at 1.34567, which is currently acting as dynamic resistance.

Conversely, the 100-period SMA (light blue) at 1.33560 has shifted from resistance to support, providing a floor for the recent price consolidation. The narrow range between these two averages suggests an imminent breakout is likely as the price searches for a definitive direction.

The path of least resistance appears slightly tilted to the downside unless the bulls can clear and close above the 1.34500 – 1.35000 resistance cluster. A rejection at the 200 SMA could lead to a retest of the 1.3333 support.

However, if the price holds above the 100 SMA, we may see further consolidation before a breakout attempt.

GBP/USD H4 Chart, March 25, 2026

Original Post

data.title: BHK: Ravaged By Higher Rates, Will Remain Under Pressure
data.description:
Thesis

We have covered the BlackRock Core Bond Trust (BHK) extensively in the past, having owned it as well for a period of time. The CEF is an interesting fund, representing a leveraged exposure to U.S. rates. In essence BHK does well when rates move lower, and gets eviscerated when yields spike higher.

In our prior coverage we also spoke about a rights offering for the name last year, corporate action which put further pressure on the CEF's price. We are re-visiting the fund after almost a year, highlighting for readers the impact of the Iran war on this name, and our take on its forward.

Fund composition - has switched to more corporate credit

Compared to the last time we covered the CEF, the fund has switched to more corporate exposure:

The 'US Government Related' bucket and the 'Agency Mortgages' bucket now make up roughly 35% of the fund, down significantly from almost 50% in the past. The CEF has a sizable corporate credit segment via the 'Investment Grade Credit' sleeve, and sizable high yield component that comes to 18%.

From a ratings standpoint roughly 80% of the fund is investment grade:

So we are getting the picture of an investment grade CEF which is now overweight corporate credit, although it still has a sizable treasuries/agencies segment. The CEF comes with a very high leverage ratio of 33.9%, and thus subject to significant volatility. Additional analytics for the CEF are as follows:

  • AUM: $700 M
  • Standard Deviation: 9%
  • Distribution rate: 9.8%
  • Duration: 9.8 years
  • Portfolio YTM: 6.4%
  • Discount to NAV: -6%

The fund has a high distribution rate, but as we shall see shortly that figure is not supported by underlying cash flows. The CEF has also increased its duration profile, with the fund now targeting the longer end of the curve with a 9.8 years duration.

Ravaged by higher rates

The CEF moves lower in price when 10-year yields spike. As of late BHK has seen negative price moves:

In the past six months the CEF is down -8%, versus -2.6% for the iShares iBoxx Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and -1.6% for the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF). LQD is a corporates only IG ETF (i.e. no leverage), while IEF is treasuries only with a 7.4 years duration. LQD also has a similar duration of roughly 8 years. Leverage plays a big role for BHK, moving the price significantly lower on yield spikes.

As long as yields are under pressure, BHK will underperform, especially when investment grade corporate spreads are also coming off a low base.

Premium/Discount to NAV

As we often see with CEF products, a poor performance in the fund translates into a widening of the discount to NAV:

The CEF is now trading at a -5.8% discount to NAV, towards the bottom of its historic range. Expect this valuation metric to remain stretched as long as oil is bid higher and yields are under pressure. As we can see from the above graph, the only time in the recent history when the CEF was at a premium was in October 2024 when the market was pricing in a very high number of Fed cuts and rates were lower.

In the case of a recession or a risk-off market, this figure can go even lower, especially during stagflation worries risk-off markets. Stagflation manifests itself via lower equity prices but higher yields, as the market tries to deal with the inflationary impact of higher oil prices.

Impact of Iran war

The Iran war is inflationary, with higher oil prices moving yield curves up across the globe. A prolonged war that results in significant infrastructure destruction and thus forces less volume even after the conflict ends is inflationary in nature. The more the conflict drags on and destroys oil infrastructure, the higher the long end of government curves. The picture is very clear for all central banks:

Top central banks have said they stand ready to tackle any surge in inflation with tighter policy as the latest escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran puts the Middle East's vital energy infrastructure in the line of fire, pushing fuel prices higher. In a rare coincidence of the monetary policy diary, central banks of the United States, Japan, Britain, Canada and the euro zone – effectively the Group of Seven (G7) nations — convened this week, as have counterparts from several emerging economies.

We saw the impact to the U.S. 10-year yields immediately:

Yields have moved higher from late December 2025, from roughly 4% to now 4.34%. Inflationary pressures are building up, thus moving rates higher to account for that metric.

Distribution is not supported

Although the CEF has a stated distribution rate of almost 10%, that figure is not supported:

An astounding 41% of the February 2026 distribution was composed of return of capital for the CEF. We do not like figures above 20% to 25% when it comes to ROC because they are long term destructive.

The fund uses a very high leverage ratio to juice up its returns, but given the portfolio yield of only 6.4%, it is quite hard to get to a sustained 10% distribution rate. Things are not going to improve here unless we have a massive dislocation in credit spreads (i.e. credit spreads higher) which would create the opportunity to pick up assets at a higher portfolio yield. Unfortunately for the CEF, such a scenario would mean much lower prices for the BHK equity.

Conclusion

BHK is a fixed income closed end fund from Blackrock. The CEF represents an aggregation of treasuries, agencies and IG corporate bonds, and comes with a very high leverage ratio of 33%. The main risk here is represented by higher rates, with the CEF under pressure from the inflationary worries coming from the Iran war. The fund's distribution is not supported, using an enormous 40% ROC in its last distribution. We see this CEF under pressure in the current macro environment and are a 'Sell' for the fund.

data.title: Interparfums: Not Enough Appeal For An Upgrade
data.description:
The last several months have been absolutely brutal for shareholders of Interparfums (IPAR). Back in June of last year, I decided to downgrade the stock from a "Buy" to a "Hold." This decision was the result of the share price of the company continuing to underperform the market. Sadly, because of fundamental performance, the stock price was no longer as attractive as it was previously. If I could go back in time, I would have downgraded it further to a "Hold" because, since the time that article was published, shares have plummeted 28.3%. Over that same window of time, the S&P 500 is up 10.5%.

The good news is that certain fundamental metrics are getting better. Revenue and net profits have been rising recently. But cash flows have been largely negative. From a valuation standpoint, the stock is starting to look a bit more attractive, especially because of the decline in share price. But even with this factored in, shares are not yet cheap enough to justify an upgrade. This means that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the most logical approach.

Taking a fresh whiff of Interparfums

If you're new to Interparfums, then it's worth noting that the company operates in the fragrance business. Specifically, it produces and sells prestige fragrances and fragrance-related products. These operations are spread throughout the United States and Europe, with Europe accounting for the largest portion of the company's revenue. In fact, last year, about 68% of its sales came from Europe, with the company benefiting from certain prestige brands that it controls, such as Kate Spade, Karl Lagerfeld, Coach, Jimmy Choo, and others. Although the European operations of the business might sound like they cater only to Europe, the company actually distributes its products to more than 120 nations worldwide from this segment. The rest of sales, meanwhile, come from the United States, where the company sells its goods under trademarks owned by it or licensed by it. Examples here include Abercrombie & Fitch, Donna Karan/DKNY, GUESS, Graff, Emanuel Ungaro, and more.

The space that the company plays in is drastically larger than what you might think it is. According to the company, the industry was worth about $57 billion in 2024. About 57% of revenue in this space involves prestige and luxury offerings. And this is where the company specializes. What's more, the industry should continue to grow for the foreseeable future. It's expected to expand by 6% annually from 2025 through 2034. If that turns out to be true, that would mean that by 2034 it will have grown to $102.1 billion in size. However, management is not focused just on this space broadly. It is especially interested in the retail segment of it, which accounts for about 75% of the overall market opportunity. Total retail sales for its brands were estimated at about $4.5 billion in 2024. And that represented a 10% increase over what it had been the year prior.

Management has succeeded in continuing to grow revenue for the company. Take the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. During that time, sales amounted to $386.2 million. That's a nice increase over the $361.5 million that the company reported a year earlier. What's really great here is that management stressed that this was even though certain headwinds remain problematic for the company in key markets. It also has the headwind of trade de-stocking that is continuing following inventory builds and in response to broader economic weakness. Management did point out that, during 2025, the business benefited from strength in a couple of core brands. These would be Jimmy Choo and Coach, which grew 6% and 15%, respectively, year over year. Rising demand for other brands such as GUESS and Montblanc also pushed revenue higher.

As revenue increased, profitability for the company grew. Net income popped from $24.2 million to $28.1 million. We also saw operating cash flow expand from $137.9 million to $146.5 million. Unfortunately, however, if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $41.9 million to $33.3 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company fell from $46.2 million to $34.1 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. The aforementioned strength in key markets pushed revenue higher. Management mentioned that results in Europe were particularly robust, with sales during that time shooting up 5.2% year over year. This was even though certain markets, such as the Middle East and Africa, took a hit. Distribution challenges in South Korea and India also proved problematic, though the company did see growth elsewhere, such as in Australia, China, and Japan. Cash flows were mostly weaker during the year, unfortunately. But that appears to be par for the course considering how the company performed during the final quarter.

When it comes to 2026, the picture for the business is expected to worsen some. Revenue is expected to fall from $1.49 billion last year to $1.48 billion this year. This is even with additional product launches planned and innovations such as the company's first ultra-luxury offering known as Solferino. In January of this year, the company entered into a 20-year license agreement with Nautica to promote fragrances and fragrance-related products under that brand. The company did the same with David Beckham. However, these will not have an immediate impact on financial performance. Nautica won't become effective until January of 2030 unless its existing license agreement is terminated. And for David Beckham, that date is April 1st of 2028. One thing that is beneficial for the company is that, in December of last year, it succeeded in renewing its license agreement for the GUESS brand that was supposed to expire in 2033. It now extends out to 2048.

In the near term, we don't have any big worries when it comes to license expirations. Technically, the next one is of the Anna Sui brand, which is supposed to expire at the end of this year. However, the company does have a single five-year optional term extension to that. We also have an expiration pending for Boucheron. But that's not until the end of 2027, followed by the March 2028 expiration of Abercrombie & Fitch and the expiration of its license with Hollister that same month. The good news is that, excluding these, the company does not have any expirations that aren't covered by optional term renewals until the end of 2029. This provides the company some protection for the foreseeable future.

Unfortunately, broader market weakness will counteract the efforts that the company is making to grow. And unfortunately, we are likely to see overall profitability decline as a result. If we take management's estimate for earnings per share for 2026 as an example, this would mean net profits of $155.6 million. That's down from the $168.4 million reported for last year. In all likelihood, this would translate to adjusted operating cash flow of $217.7 million and to EBITDA of $272.9 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the results the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. These multiples are certainly not bad, but I wouldn't say that they put the company in the undervalued range either. In that same chart, you can see how the business stacks up against other similar enterprises. While only one of these companies is cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis, three of them are cheaper when it comes to the other two ways of valuing it.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available, I believe that Interparfums makes for an interesting company. But it's not one that I think is worthy of a bullish rating at this time. Broader economic conditions are proving to be a challenge for it, and the stock is not cheap enough to justify anything other than a "Hold" at this time.

data.title: Magnite: Managing The Balance Between The Legacy Decline And CTV Growth
data.description:
Based in the US, Magnite (MGNI) is one of the largest advertising companies providing a sell-side or supply side (SSP) platform to enable publishers monetize their advertising space across channels, such as connected TV (CTV), desktop, or mobile. MGNI was initially founded as Rubicon Project back in 2007, but it went through a series of venture capital funding and corporate actions over the years, and following a merger with another company Telaria, it changed its name into Magnite.

Today, MGNI has two main revenue streams, the CTV, its primary growth engine that includes products such as ClearLine and LiveScheduler, and DV+, its legacy business that includes its mobile and desktop ads platform. As of Q4 2025, CTV business has made up almost half of the total business and been the fastest-growing segment for MGNI.

Since going public, MGNI has seen significant volatility, peaking at around $61 per share during the 2021 ad-tech surge, but then facing a sharp decline over the next year and traded as low as $7 per share in late 2022. Though the stock has been on track for a recovery, with some momentum towards Q4 last year as the stock reached a 1-year high of $26 per share around August, share performance has been weak YTD, with the stock most recently trading around $12 per share, down -25% within the same period.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $13.7 suggests that MGNI appears undervalued.

Financial Reviews

Based on the latest Q4 2025 results, I found MGNI’s fundamentals improving. The underlying top-line growth, for instance, has been healthy for the most part. In Q4, MGNI saw a revenue of $205.4 million, a 6% YoY growth, which while seems minimal, does not tell the whole story just yet.

However, if we zoom in to the metric that measures the net revenue for an SSP like MGNI, the contribution ex-TAC, it appears that MGNI delivered a double-digit 16% YoY growth when the impact from cyclical political advertising is removed. From an FY standpoint, we see a similar picture. MGNI delivered an FY contribution ex-TAC of $670 million, a 10% YoY growth. Excluding the political ads, however, MGNI would have seen a 14% YoY growth instead.

Improvements are seen across the board as well. Profitability, for instance, saw a decent improvement as MGNI realized an adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) of $83.8 million, a 9% YoY growth, meaning that MGNI has been able to maintain its aEBITDA margin at the same level YoY, demonstrating a steady performance. On a FY basis, MGNI even saw a much stronger 18% YoY aEBITDA growth, which further suggests a solid operating leverage in its business model.

Liquidity has been robust as well as of Q4 2025, with MGNI ending the year with $553 million in cash and equivalents, an increase from $483.2 million the year prior. Meanwhile, MGNI is also a cash-flow positive company, with operating cash flow (OCF) of $61 million in Q4 2025. Another kye highlight in Q4 is MGNI’s improved net leverage, which the company brought down from 0.3x last quarter to zero, as the management commented in the earnings call:

Net leverage for the quarter was 0, down from 0.3x at the end of Q3. As a reminder, the remaining $205 million principal balance of our convertible notes is a current liability on the balance sheet as the notes mature this quarter. We plan to pay off the converts at maturity with cash on hand next month.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Catalyst

I believe catalysts should remain quite solid into 2026, driven by both the continued growth in the programmatic CTV advertising industry as a whole.

With MGNI continuing to focus on CTV as its primary growth driver, I foresee revenue mix further shifting towards CTV, increasing its share of revenue potentially beyond 50% into the FY. This would be a positive thing for MGNI, since CTV remains a premium, higher-margin segment, suggesting that the higher revenue share in CTV should continue to drive margin expansion, as already seen in FY 2025.

Overall, the double-down in CTV seems to be supported by the favorable overall industry dynamics, as suggested by the management in Q4 earnings call:

The long anticipated ramp of programmatic CTV is no longer emerging. It is underway at scale. Adoption is broad-based across media owners, agencies and DSPs. We saw strong growth from many of the largest players in the industry, including LG Ads, Netflix, Paramount, Roku, VIZIO, Walmart and Warner Bros. Discovery. TV OEMs are leaning aggressively into programmatic across home screens, pause ads, data enablement and marketplaces. Programmatic enablement in live sports continues to expand across the largest global streamers.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

With the consumption of CTV content growing, driven by the fact that 90% of US households have at least one CTV device, according to adwave, then the CTV advertising growth becomes more of a natural consequence, in my opinion.

Nielsen's data further solidifies such a point of view. Based on the data, we will expect a significantly larger share of marketers increasing their spend in CTV or OTT in the US, MGNI’s largest market, in the next twelve months as per 2025 data. In my view, the significant jump here further suggests that the trend may as well continue into FY 2026, benefitting MGNI as a result, before it normalizes in the future.

Risk

Overall, I believe the risk to be minimal to moderate. As in any companies dealing with declining legacy business I have covered before, managing the balance between the growing and the declining segments becomes a crucial part in driving market confidence. For instance, while we should expect MGNI’s DV+ revenue to decline into FY 2026, by then it would still definitely make up a significant share of MGNI’s business. As such, if DV+ segment sees a higher-than-expected revenue decline to the extent that it significantly offsets the overall revenue growth, then a potential correction may not be surprising in my opinion.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for MGNI is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the consensus analysis:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I assume CTV growth growing above 30%, with the company successfully executing its share repurchase program up to $200 million, effectively reducing share count. I assign MGNI a forward P/S of 3x to accommodate a re-rating due to the controlled transition from a legacy ad-tech into a higher-growth CTV ad-tech firm.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I assume a higher-than-expected DV+ decline that offsets CTV growth, forcing the management to pause share buyback as it tries to troubleshoot its legacy business decline. I expect the market to maintain its view that MGNI remains a legacy ad-tech firm as transition sees hiccups. I assign a 2.5x forward P/S, aligning with its TTM P/S.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $13.7 per share, implying a 14% upside. At this point, I give MGNI a buy rating.

Conclusion

Similarly to many other tech businesses I covered in the past with legacy issues, I believe MGNI today is at a critical inflection point, where one of its businesses is growing while the other one deemed as a legacy segment is declining. It continues to see success in its higher-margin CTV business as of Q4, as the growth in that segment hit 32%, driving the management to focus on growing the CTV business into the FY.

My opinion is that the industry dynamics today is generally favorable towards the move, and in paper, it should enable MGNI to see CTV growth continuation that will drive margin expansion as well, as we have seen in FY 2025. However, on the other hand, I believe that the impact of the declining legacy business, DV+, should not be underestimated. My price target analysis factors in all these components, and even with my most conservative assumption of 50-50 bull-bear scenario probability, it appears that MGNI appears undervalued today.

data.title: How To Navigate Stagflation Fears: Seeking Less Correlated Assets
data.description:
Why Stagflation Is a Nightmare for Investors

From gold to fixed income to equities, these typically less-correlated asset classes are now moving in the same direction, as the ongoing war in the Middle East shows no signs of easing. The length of the war has exceeded market expectations. We've seen a spike in the VIX index, with Trump's mixed tweets adding further volatility.

Over the past two weeks, we saw chaos in the Middle East. Missiles have been flying across the region, attacking energy and oil refinery infrastructures. At the same time, the prolonged disruption of key export routes, such as the Strait of Hormuz, has caused a major supply shock in the oil market.

Brent crude prices saw squeezing, with the spread to WTI widening to a decade high. In addition. The prices of physical oil are much higher than what the paper markets indicate, showing that oil futures are trading at a significant discount.

The market is increasingly anxious about elevated oil prices and how they could feed into the global economy and inflation expectations in the coming months. Since last Friday, the bond market has been selling off sharply. Long-term treasuries, including TLT, even underperformed the Nasdaq index. The market initially priced in a 50% chance of a rate hike this year, which is a clear sign of stagflation fears.

How do we deal with a stagflation environment? Unfortunately, we don't have many places to hide. For equities, I believe it's premature to call a bottom after a 5% YTD selloff. A typical recession would push the S&P 500 index (SP500, NYSEARCA:SPY) into a bear market (a -20% peak-to-trough decline). Furthermore, the U.S. equity valuation still looks stretched, so I maintain my Hold rating on SPY for now. However, I think short- to medium-term fixed income looks more attractive after major pullbacks.

The "Trump Put" Could Still Happen

As I pointed out back in April 2025, "Trump Put" could still help lift the market sentiment this time, even though the current geopolitical tensions may be beyond his control.

On Monday morning around 7am EST, oil prices tanked 10%, and the major indexes rebounded nearly 4% from overnight losses after Trump tweeted that the U.S. had temporarily backed off from attacking Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure and extended the deadline by five days.

Trump is unlikely to risk the U.S. economy ahead of the midterms later this year. If necessary, we should expect more constructive tweets from him to support the market sentiment, even if the war drags on. However, major U.S. indexes were still closed below 200 MAs. From a technical perspective, we are not out of the woods yet.

Treasuries Could Lose Hedging Ability

Under a stagflation backdrop, the correlation between SPY and TLT becomes more positive compared to a low inflation regime, as the Fed will not rush to cut interest rates when the economy cracks.

On March 20th, the bond market had priced in a 50% probability of a 25 bps rate hike in October this year. We see the odds have now declined to 25% on March 24th, following Trump's tweet on Monday morning. Even so, this is a sharp reversal from the expected 50 bps cuts before the war started.

With stagflation fears rising, the key question is how long the oil supply shock will last, especially since an economic slowdown could offset some inflationary pressure through weaker demand. Lower inflation should eventually allow TLT to outperform again, despite U.S. fiscal deficit issues.

Although this might seem like a contrarian call, I believe the risk and reward of extending bond duration now looks more attractive than buying the dip in SPY at current levels. That doesn't mean you need to go all in on the long end of the yield curve. I know some may stick to the "be greedy when others are fearful" mindset. Given that the 20-year Treasury yield is hovering around 5%, it makes sense to lock in higher yields while waiting for more clarity. If inflation expectations later ease during a recession, treasuries could have additional appreciation benefits.

Lately, we've also seen credit spreads widen. I believe this is largely due to the ongoing war and spillover from the recent private credit events. BBB OAS has climbed up but is still significantly below the distressed levels back in 2020 and 2022. Adding some investment-grade credit exposures can yield more than 5% without overly extending duration.

S&P 500 Forward P/E Still Looks Rich

According to the recent FactSet report, the S&P 500's forward P/E is at 20.3x, still above both its 5-year and 10-year averages. I know that AI spending in 2026 remains strong, and the recent earnings results from hyperscalers and semiconductor companies have been very solid. If the war causes a stagflation, economic growth and corporate spending will slow, which means that these large-scale AI investments could be delayed. This would lower the forward earnings consensus and make the S&P 500's forward P/E even more expensive.

Compared to the Russian-Ukraine war back in February 2022, global rates were much lower. The U.S. was still running QE at that time, with rates near 0%. Now with a 4% rate environment, the current backdrop is more fragile, as a prolonged oil supply shock could have a much bigger impact on the economy. Also keep in mind that the S&P 500's earnings revisions usually have a lag.

Nevertheless, any panic selloffs in the S&P 500 will eventually create a strong buying opportunity, but we are not there yet. If you ask me, I would be more bullish if the forward P/E falls below the 10-year average or if the VIX index approaches the 40 to 50 levels.

What If You Are a Risk-Averse Investor?

For risk-averse investors, you may focus on low-volatility assets. As mentioned earlier, we've seen selloffs across many asset classes. Oil prices show strong upside momentum, but speculating at this level could be risky, as price movements are largely driven by daily headline news.

If inflation expectations move higher, U.S. TIPS, or (TIP), could be an option, as you will receive more interest as inflation picks up. We can see that the current 30-day SEC yield is around 4.2% with an effective duration of 6.4 years. Its equity beta is only 0.17, which doesn't show a strong correlation with equities compared to long-term treasuries.

Another simple solution is just holding 3-month U.S. treasuries, such as (SGOV). SGOV only has 0.1 years of effective duration and nearly 0 equity beta. You would likely see a positive P/L on a daily basis as SGOV accumulates dividends (except for dividend payout dates when the accrued dividend is excluded from the price). Therefore, by holding 3-month treasuries on the sidelines, you still can earn a decent yield, and the yield will go higher if the Fed raises interest rates later this year. This gives you even more flexibility to wait for better opportunities to deploy your cash into equities.

Conclusion

In summary, while a Trump Put could still help support market sentiment, I expect equity volatility to remain elevated in the near term as the war continues. Given the equity market's rich valuation and potential economic damage from higher oil prices, SPY could face further downside. If a full-blown recession materializes, it could ease some demand and inflation pressure, so long-term treasuries such as TLT can regain some hedging ability against equity selloffs. Therefore, I believe extending duration and gradually adding TLT exposure looks attractive for risk-taking investors. For risk-averse investors, holding some cash or SGOV is not a bad idea, as higher rates can offer higher yields on 3-month treasuries.

From a portfolio allocation perspective, in a distressed market, assets that are usually less or even negatively correlated can suddenly move together. That's why having assets with negative correlation becomes much more valuable during stagflation.

data.title: Investing On Both Sides Of The K-Shaped Economy
data.description:
Commentators have been discussing the “K-Shaped Economy” for a few years, but as time has gone on, it's only gotten worse. What I naively thought would be a trend that we left behind post-2023 after the worst of the inflation woes ended has become a long-term trend. The upper end is doing okay, whereas the middle class and lower end are weakening. We've noticed it in wage growth primarily, but it's also in asset growth, as well as other factors like default rates.

I've been covering this trend on Seeking Alpha, most recently in “The Consumer is Tapping Out,” where I discussed flatlining retail sales. Here's an excerpt making a point about the recent acceleration of the lower leg of the K.

....Placer Labs, which tracks foot traffic into retail stores...noted that discount and dollar stores like Dollar General (DG) and Dollar Tree (DLTR), among others, had [risen above superstore traffic]. It would seem that consumer spending was flat because they were trading down on the price chain to afford similar quantities.

They noted similar trends among off-price retailers—above the dollar stores in the price-to-quantity ratio but below superstores—and thrift stores as well, with their traffic also spiking during the peak of this holiday shopping season compared to last year.

The consumer becoming more money-conscious is not a good sign of their robustness. I was quick to dismiss the retail sales data being flat as noisy, especially considering that 2024 was an unusual year in wage growth, so comparisons are tough. But take in the data that off-price, thrift, and dollar stores are taking market share of consumers while the YoY rate is flat, and it looks like contraction to me.

The question was put to me recently, asking what stocks I'd consider buying to benefit from this trend. Now that I think we're entering a zone of market support that will mark a near-term bottom, it's worth really considering.

Luxury Has Been Repricing

The high end has been hit hard as this change in economic trajectory has caused consumers to tighten their purses, especially the middle class that was increasingly a target audience for brand conglomerates like LVMH (LVMUY). Their top line took a steep turn following 2023.

They are but one of many firms that have seen sales drop, customers move down the value chain, and their stocks have similarly suffered. It hit these firms across the board, and to varying degrees, during a fairly persistent bull market.

Middle consumer brands are suffering from this same trend. The folks trading down from Lululemon to Nike (NKE) aren't replacing the folks trading down from Nike to a pair of no-name sneakers off a Chinese-based vendor on Amazon.com (AMZN).

This is where prudent investors will need to separate the wheat from the chaff and really look at which firms are worth scooping up at these levels.

With LVMH's forward PE at 20, it's become far more tempting as an addition to my portfolio, but I'm still unsure where this bleed will end. It's gone on longer than I initially anticipated, so I'm not invested in this side of the trade yet.

The Low End Repriced In Kind

The money that left the higher-end retailers went to the other side of the K. Not only did the market cap shift, but revenues did too. The S&P 500 gets lost in this crowd, running far above it. That does make this end of the trade pricier, but it's also the side with more momentum.

Note that dollar stores, while I was positive about their foot traffic in the beginning of the article, have not been particularly great businesses to invest in over the last few years.

These may be the real hidden gems on the low end of the market—although regular readers know I am deeply fond of Costco (COST) and have given it several buy ratings—as they are firms that can take advantage of the K-shaped economic trend while not having been bought up by the market yet.

My colleague Kenio Fontes recently covered Five Below (FIVE) and gave it a buy rating. I found it compelling and suggest investors interested in the stock read his recent article. It is a good place to start for this segment.

The Biggest Winner Is Not A Retailer

My focus here has been on a firm that I think is planning to win on both fronts. Instead of catering to premium or discount shoppers, why not do both?

Apple (AAPL) has been busy this month, releasing eight new products.

The main focus is the new MacBook Neo, which will be the cheapest MacBook ever launched, hitting shelves at $599. Neo comes in at a cool $200 less than the 2014 MacBook Air launched at. This is a considerable shift in target audience for Apple's laptop division.

Their smartphones had already been targeting the budget phone market since last year, when they launched the iPhone 16e. Now the 17e was announced this month; the e variants look likely to stay in future releases as well. It should be of note that the 16e got some public backlash over its pricing, as reviewers did not feel its price was low enough to be considered a budget phone.

Apple is also increasing its offering for the high end, targeting AI power users. They advertise the new M5 Max and M5 Pro laptops come with GPUs that have neural accelerators to run large language models with the onboard hardware (allowing models to be run offline). The highest-end model tops out at $3,899.

Note that Apple is one of the few firms that has not been piling into the massive CapEx spend on data centers. So, while they are making AI-forward devices, they aren't spending all of their cash flow on building out AI infrastructure. In fact, they are the only large data center operator that has decreased their CapEx rate over the last year.

Conclusion

The consumer is still bifurcating, and it's getting worse for high-end brands. While many of the stocks in the premium retail sector are beaten down, I believe that many of them are still “falling knives.” Investors that are going bargain hunting there on the repricing of luxury brands should consider how much further these firms could go, though I am finding LVMH to be attractive at these levels, and I am considering a position.

Firms that cater to budget-conscious consumers have taken the market share, and their stocks have seen strong momentum. Stores serving customers from middle and lower incomes have seen their stocks soar, and many have entered overvalued territory. Some are worthwhile holding, and I would not consider selling my shares of Costco anytime soon regardless of this market trend pushing it higher alongside other names.

Dollar stores have had a particularly rough trend over the last few years and are worth considering here as potentially lower valuation buys. I recommend considering Five Below as a starting point in the subsector.

A firm that has just made a pivot to play both sides at the same time and one that had a fairly impressive lineup of products announced this month that it expanded its catalog in both directions. Apple has garnered my interest here. I am considering taking a position in Apple alongside my other large tech holdings, as they look poised to capitalize on growing consumer trends. I recently took a short-term derivative trade on Apple but intend to establish a longer-term position soon as well.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Too Many AI Winners To Settle For Super Micro Computer
data.description:
What matters now is not whether Super Micro Computer (SMCI) can deliver at scale, but whether it is a messy winner or a structurally impaired business due to governance risk. The March 2026 smuggling case with alleged $2.5 billion in smuggled AI hardware and the arrest of co-founder Wally Liaw changes the lens by which investors think about Super Micro. Regardless of the ultimate legal outcome, the pattern here is not an isolated incident, it’s a systemic problem that’s appearing at a time when the infrastructure for AI is becoming more strategically and tightly regulated.

The Cracks Beneath the Surge

The uncomfortable fact is that Super Micro’s business was a harder story to believe in even before the legal news broke. The company reported $12.7 billion in revenue for fiscal Q2 2026, a gain of 123% year-over-year and raised its full-year sales guidance to a minimum of $40 billion.

On the surface, that’s the kind of business that investors love to invest in precisely because they think it’s too good to pass up. The problem is that the quality of the business has changed for the worse. Gross margin came in at 6.3% down from 11.8% a year ago. Cash flow from operations was negative $24 million and free cash flow was negative $45 million. The company’s inventory also climbed to $10.6 billion from $5.7 billion the prior quarter.

I am having difficulty understanding this profile and relating it to the premium story that was historically tied to this name. A company can certainly accept lower margins in a scale phase if they are achieving this scale in a manner that is resulting in sustainable customer lock-in, better working capital, or a rising software and service mix. Super Micro is attempting to prove this out with their DCBBS product, which they say accounted for 4% of their profit in their first half and had margins above 20%.

What I think is more interesting to focus on, however, is the fact that this is a relatively small offset to their core hardware business. Their core business is still very much levered to low margin, high speed server deployments and this makes them much more vulnerable to pricing and customer concentration issues than their top line alone might suggest.

When Dependence Meets Doubt

Customer concentration was a concern to begin with and it still is but I think it is a bigger concern to me following the recent legal developments than it was before them. Specifically, in their Q2, one of their larger data center customers accounted for about 63% of their overall revenue and 84% of their revenue came from their OEM appliance and large data center segment.

This was a precarious position to begin with and this precarious position was really centered around margin and execution issues. It is an even more precarious position now that their counterparties may reassess their partnership exposure in the middle of an export-sensitive situation.

This is where I think the Nvidia (NVDA) relationship becomes less comfortable than bulls think. Nvidia reaffirmed their commitment to export compliance and does not support systems that are diverted for illegal purposes. This is the right stance to take. It also tells you what you need to know. Nvidia doesn’t have an incentive to lean in harder on a partner that’s suddenly becoming a reputational and regulatory risk.

On the other hand, Dell (DELL) is not standing still. They announced quarterly AI optimized server revenue of $9 billion, up 342% year-over-year. They also announced a $43 billion AI backlog at the end of the quarter. They are also making it clear that they are the premier partner for enterprise AI infrastructure built on the Nvidia platform.

I am not saying that Nvidia walks away from Super Micro. I am saying that the burden of proof now lies with Nvidia. I would rather not be the weaker partner in that relationship. The market continues to debate the damage, but the damage is already done at a strategic level. The formal worst case may be obvious, but the real damage may be happening in ways that are harder to see and harder to quantify.

Cheap on Paper But Expensive in Assumptions

Many might fall for the valuation but that’s exactly where investors can get misled. At these price levels, you are not only assuming that the revenue base is legitimate but also that it is sustainable without significant impact from compliance remediation, customer attrition, or partner repricing. That is a lot to assume at a cheap multiple.

The reality is that the warning signs were out there all along. Margins were eroding, cash flow was weak and the customer concentration was extremely high, even before the scandal broke. The legal issue did not create the problem, it exposed underlying weaknesses that were already present. The reason the stock is cheap is not because people are ignoring a good business, but because people are discounting the uncertainty of how sustainable the revenue is and at what margins.

The reason I wouldn’t invest, even at a low multiple, is that I would like to see significant improvement in gross margins, a less concentrated customer base and significant progress on the compliance front before I believe the earnings power behind the current valuation. What would change my view of the bear case?

The actual risk to a negative view, in my opinion, isn’t that the stock goes up. It likely can. Very volatile infrastructure companies with poor fundamental narratives often have vicious rallies. The actual risk to a negative view would be that I incorrectly label a significant but transitory problem in corporate governance as a fundamental problem in the business.

Relief Rally or Real Recovery? The Burden of Proof Is Rising

If customers continue to order, Nvidia support remains functional and DCBBS really does start to lift mix and profitability over the next two quarters, then perhaps the current drawdown in the stock has been a panic selling event rather than the start of a fundamental de-rating. There are also legal factors to consider. Super Micro has claimed that it is not a defendant in this case and has cooperated with the authorities. In this case, perhaps some of the worst fears that investors have about this stock might not be realized.

However, unless gross margins start to expand significantly from their current levels, unless the 63% customer concentration issue somehow resolves itself in the future and unless revenues start to grow much more rapidly than they have in this latest report, then perhaps this would be confirmation that the business was indeed weaker than the revenues indicated even before this scandal began.

Bottom Line

I wouldn’t touch this stock. It’s a speculative play with too many unknowns and I don’t like the risk. I don’t see the need to speculate in a market that’s filled with good quality AI plays.

data.title: Salesforce: Market Is Pricing It At Doomsday Valuations
data.description:
Salesforce: Wipeout is not imminent

Back in my previous update on Salesforce (CRM), I mentioned that the company is going nowhere, that they are here to stay for the long term, and CRM's recent fourth quarter earnings highlights validate my conviction. Yet, after a short respite in early March, even as the Iranian conflict intensified, CRM stock is yet again tethering dangerously closer to those downcast February moments once more. I thought the market would have banished the ghosts of the so-called “2028 Global Intelligence Crisis” for good, but it seems like the worries haven't been fully extinguished. However, the market is having other ideas right now. They are scrutinizing software companies that have overexerted themselves in using stock based compensation, that Wall Street is now asking more questions about, as investors wonder whether their earnings quality could still hold up in a world where "AI eats software," and cause the long duration terminal value computations to be lowered significantly.

In case, you have not realized, the application software industry have gone through one of the worst hammerings in the last decade, and I think that characterization is not even close. Despite analysts upgrading their forward earnings estimates for the industry, the multiples continue to go lower through February this year, as investors digested the multitudinous bearish takes on the long-term durability and sustainability of SaaS as we know it.

Questions are now being asked whether these software companies will still be relevant in an era where we can simply vibe code, where Anthropic’s AI coding tools (ANTHRO) become even more intelligent, and capable, and, more worryingly, highly effective AI agents build software replacements for solutions provided by application SaaS companies.

It is no longer just about overlaying over the SaaS interface, but customizing with a new interface by the development teams within the company themselves. For AI pure plays, they probably see this potential market share grab as the first step in entrenching themselves into the broader enterprise workloads.

However, it is also notable that the software TAM is possibly nowhere near enough to justify the increasingly close to one trillion dollar valuation they see of OpenAI (OPENAI) right now. Ultimately, for these highly valued AI companies to survive and even justify their valuations, killing off software companies per se will not be enough, I think. They have to find a way through to disrupt the labor market in a big way to accrue value, as the labor factor or cost is arguably one of the most significant and appealing TAM for AI pure plays to go for and prove to the market that they can command increasingly higher valuations into the multi trillion dollar mark through the rest of the decade.

In a recent podcast over the weekend, Nobel Prize winning economist and New York Times bestselling author Daron Acemoglu shared helpful insights to let us understand some of the most pertinent and crucial questions about the unfolding (miracle or carnage, depending on which side you are on) artificial intelligence revolution and what it means for the economy, our society, and our precious democracy. And if you think that you are expecting something diplomatic to come out from the Nobel Prize winner, you will be surprised, as he informed us about what the AI companies could be thinking:

There is so little that these companies are doing in order to understand what work humans do and try to be useful to humans. The whole agenda of all of the leading companies in the United States and now joined by DeepSeek in China is AGI, artificial general intelligence. That is a banner for saying these models are going to do everything better than humans. Which of course then leads to the corollary that a lot of companies should just throw away their humans and use these companies. That is an automation agenda. - Bloomberg podcast on “AI Is Being Built to Replace You—Not Help You”

Salesforce is built for the AI agentic era

The way I see it right now, I don't see that companies are forsaking spending on the leading software companies like Salesforce imminently. CRM owns its space. The company is still estimated to deliver $46.1 billion in revenue for FY2027, and CRM’s long-term outlook suggests that the road to $63 billion is possible, despite all the disruption fears going on right now.

Although the earnings revisions for Salesforce are mixed, they are not downright gloomy, and I think that's what you need to know. As long as the outlook is mixed, it should give us some confidence (at the very least) that unless we are looking at a true apocalypse, all the software stocks, including the best ones with tremendous moats, should not face immediate or even medium-term wipeout. We should try not to be too pessimistic at this moment and think that every company out there is going to throw away/discard Salesforce from their suite of enterprise software and go into vibe coding it themselves.

Furthermore, the $800 million for Agentforce AI ARR gives me confidence that the company is making some progress. Could that acceleration be faster? Of course, why not? Salesforce definitely has the potential to do a lot better. We all do. But the market appears to be looking for constant validation after validation and is not willing to put faith into Salesforce long term numbers.

After all, the company has even derived a new (buzzword) called agentic work unit, or AWU. Through this new development, Salesforce wants us to know that companies are deploying AI agents through its platform already, and accelerating.

Remember, for AI tools to work, they require context, they require data, and they require an adaptive system that helps companies navigate which AI tools for which solution. So, Salesforce has all of this orchestrated on its ecosystem.

Can any company simply just override almost decades of data and learning from vibe coding? I'm really not so sure if they can, even if they would like to. They are intelligent tools, but they are not almighty, right? They still need to plug in to Salesforce, and you can bet that Salesforce is not going to let these companies pipe out their data for free, absolutely no way.

They will have to pay the toll, right? There goes my next point. Well, the seat base pricing model could come under more pressure, as enterprise companies use the new agentic coding opportunities to review their contracts, or to delay upgrading to more expensive or premium solutions offered by Salesforce.

But, the company also has the potential to monetize the tokens, or in this case the AWUs, over time. In Salesforce parlance, AWU represents the work done by the AI agents, offering additional and more helpful insights as compared to just tokens, which primarily represent spending.

Therefore, I believe it gives investors a more helpful and broader overview about Salesforce's ability to price consumption-based models accordingly, based on the value that they offer to the enterprise customers. We shouldn't assume that Salesforce will be short-changed just because they face tremendous pressure, as we read, or when companies appear to want to reduce their spending, even though that hasn't really happened because I don't see any assessments for Salesforce being written down in unison across Wall Street, as I enunciated earlier.

Buy Or Sell CRM Stock?

With that in mind, I find it really incredulous that CRM is trading at the valuation that makes it look absolutely pathetic, but also not justified in my view. At just under 14x forward earnings, has the market gone bonkers, valuing one of the most profitable software companies with such a solid moat at well under the S&P 500’s 20x metric?

It’s also significantly below the SaaS average of 22.7, representing not just a simple disconnect, but I believe an unlikely doomsday scenario that the thought experiment relating to what the "2028 Global Intelligence Crisis" wants us to believe.

This is the moment where we must take courage, dive in, and be willing to embrace opportunity when all the rest just see risks. It wouldn't be an easy ride, and it wouldn't be a smooth one either. But hang on tight, be willing to wait patiently, and the mean reversion will pan out over time, I believe.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Burke & Herbert Post-Merger In 2026 - Not My First Pick
data.description:
I've been looking more into regional US banks as of late - in search of value without too much correlation to other factors. While some regional banking institutions have too much CRE exposure or too many factors that make them risky by their own right, there are some gems that I've uncovered worthy of investing in at the right price. And some that are worth investing in right now. Today, I'll look at the result of a merger which I covered about 2 years ago and more - Burke & Herbert (BHRB). My last article on the company can be found here.

While the results of the merger (at the time, the company seemed to be about to grow more) have been going well, the bank has also been going up and down quite a it. For the past year, the total RoR is less than 5.5%, and for the 10-year period, BHRB has managed an RoR of only 26.6%.

This is generally less than the overall financial sector, and it's certainly a lot less than my own personal RoR. This implies to me that BHRB is quite volatile, and likely (barring changes), to continue to be somewhat volatile.

However, there's a set of estimates currently relevant that put the company at an upside for the next 2-3 years. It's my intention with this article to clarify this potential upside, to determine its likelihood, and to work out what the company could be worth as of March 2026. I will note, however, that the company, compared to other regional banks that I cover, tends to be smaller (in terms of market capitalization). A smaller size can be both positive and negative. I will speak to both sides of this equation here.

Let's start with the positives.

Burke & Herbert - Upside requires an outperformance, not just valuation recovery

From the get-go, and from my last article, I called this company a "speculative" investment. In this context, it means that I did not invest in the business. Burke & Herbert is now a regional banking play with a market capitalization just shy of $950M. This makes it relatively small. The company does not come with an S&P Global credit rating, and it comes with a yield of 3.56% at a current P/E multiple of 8x. This makes it lower valued than most regional peers, but it also makes the yield at this multiple lower than its regional and national (as well as international) peers.

If you were "with me" on my last coverage of regional banks, Huntington (HBAN), you will have noted that I spend a lot of energy speaking to asset quality, NPL's, NPA's, and the like to determine the quality of a bank or financial institution that I look at. Unfortunately, this is also where BHRB shows its different colors to some of its peers - and this is unfortunate, because this can have a lot of reasons (and few of them are good).

BRHB, as of the latest results, has shown clear indicators of deterioration in loan and asset quality. NPL's are up to 1.6% in 3Q25 (down again in 4Q), which is more than 2.5x that of HBAN. Compare it to a major bank in Scandinavia, and it's 50-100x higher - but given that we're looking at regionals, we'll keep things regional and US-based. This increase in "bad loans" was from a 0.64% about 2 years ago.

This rise brings to light a few other risks, because changes here are interconnected. The company's reserve coverage is dropping. Allowances for credit losses (as % of NPL's) are now down to less than 76%. This means that these allowances don't necessarily cover the NPLs, which implies that the bank may be (not certain, it may, depending on development) underreserved for defaults.

Unfortunately, unlike HBAN, BHRB has a high exposure to CRE. This is the thing I liked HBAN for not having, and it's therefore, on the same logic, something I will mark BHRB down for. The unfortunate part is that, which I also pointed out in my latest article, the merger with Summit has actually increased the commercial real estate exposure of the bank. HBAN has less than 15%. BRHB has more than 55% of its loans in CRE, which comes to over 310% of RBC (risk-based capital).

What exactly does this concentration mean to you as an investor?

It means that if you want to invest in BHRB, you should be very knowledgeable about the CRE sector, specifically in the bank's focal areas - otherwise, you may be swimming in very deep waters indeed.

This sort of exposure means that over half of the banks' loans would suffer if the CRE sector was to be revaluated, or if interest rates were to go up. All of these things would increase the refinancing risk. And the thing is, with the way things seem to be going right now, I do not believe it to be illogical to expect either a revaluation of CRE in the company's focal areas, or, for that matter, an interest in interest rates eventually.

Now, those bullish on BHRB and its exposures would refer to the DMV focus (DC, Maryland, VA), and that property values here generally have been stable or going up, with Maryland here as an example.

My counterpoint to this argument would be that the deterioration in loan quality and NPL's implies to me that the CRE covering those loans is bad, because the NPL itself reflects a failure to meet payments, which in itself is either a sign of a bad lessee or a poor quality CRE - neither of which is a positive.

I therefore say that BHRB should be considered at the very least a higher-risk bank than others, and the market dictating valuation seems to agree.

Then there's the potential merger of LINKBANCORP (LNKB), which I am not a fan of. I've read up on it for the past few days, and it's not something I consider a positive move.

Why don't I like the prospective merger with LINKBANKCORP?

Because the target in this case is a bank that lost over 35% of its market capitalization over the past 4 years. This is also, given the bank's own size, a big acquisition. If it was done 4 years ago, it would be closer to a 50% to the company's own size - and even today, it's a $350M+ M&A, which comes to a third of the size of BHRB.

What also speaks against it is in this deal, the company is taking on a significant (10% of market cap) amount of goodwill. They are further taking on this amount of risk at a time when their own loan quality is sliding. The combined prospective bank will not have a lower, but higher exposure to CRE, particularly in spaces and subsectors that are already subject to vacancies (such as office). Despite what I would consider an equally poor asset quality, implied also by the loss of 40% of share value in 4 years, BHRB is in my view, also overpaying for the bank. At about 1.5x tangible book, the risk is certainly at least worth mentioning that it's overpaying for underperforming assets - unless BHRB knows something we don't about the assets.

But even if that is true, then the M&A will have an immediate negative effect on the company's KPI's. It will, with very little doubt, increase the cost of deposits and decrease its capital ratios, with a CET-1 dropping at the very least 100 bps, and BHRB's CET-1 pre this merger was actually one of its saving graces, because it stands at almost 13%, which is very good for US regional banking.

The goodwill is especially worrying because it amounts to more than 2.5x the amount of goodwill that the company currently holds on its balance sheet. The reason I don't like excess goodwill in a merger I assume, to be of questionable quality is that the risk of impairment charges is high. Remember, Goodwill is the premium paid above the fair market value of tangibles and intangibles - and this is only relevant if the business performs the expected synergies or upside. I expect the risk is high that it, in fact, does not do this.

I believe BRHB's historical performance is indicative of the potential underperformance of this M&A as well, which is why I would demand a significant discount to be interested in investing here.

Do we have this discount?

Burke & Herbert Valuation - discount is there, but it may not be enough.

We have a discount.

Current valuation for the bank is, as I mentioned, about 8x P/E. This is low for regional banking, which trends towards 9-12x P/E. However, as I believe that I have established above, it's not unfair to be worried about BHRB's near-term performance and to question the background and potential of its current merger.

But there is one thing that may justify an investment here. A massive EPS increase. You see, the current analyst average is a 17% AEPS growth in 2027, another 16% in 2028E.

Why?

To understand that, we go back to the positive side of the merger. The bank expects significant cost savings from this merger, as well as expansion into new markets, such as PA, and other states. BHRB will attempt to use the high scale to leverage efficiencies, and current expectations are for an EPS growth of over 30% in total. This also explains the buy-side rationale behind the merger, and I can respect this. Combine such an upside with the leveraging of digital technology, and we may see something like this.

In the event of this sort of earnings growth, even a low-end 8.5x P/E on a forward basis would imply a share price of well over $85/share for 2028E, which would in turn make a strong case for a "BUY" rating here, easily clearing my hurdle of 15% annualized.

The problem I am having with this way of viewing the thesis is that it relies entirely (because it currently trades at 8x P/E) on the realization of that growth. And because of a number of factors, I do not consider that growth to be as easily accessible or realized as the bank or others may expect.

The combination of risks for NPL's, for lower reserves, for CRE exposure, for margin increases (remember, margins went to 34.2% in 4Q, how likely is it that another surge is coming? I say not highly), historical EPS decline rates, with persistent integration challenges with Summit, and with bank-technical risks like AFS securities losses following interest hikes (which is part of my thesis), creates a situation where I do not just want growth-related upside, I actually want reversal upside as well.

I therefore say that the bank is not cheap enough for me yet, and I add my rating to the "HOLD" currently given by both recent SA as well as Quant.

What PT?

With the reservation that this is still a speculative investment, I do not believe that my previously given P/E premium (post-Summit) of 10-12x P/E is justified here. I'd want my hurdle rate to be a conservative P/E valuation on a forward basis. Working from a 25% discount rate for the 2028E results, which I believe to be fair based on the risks mentioned above, and a lower-end 9.5x P/E fair value for the bank calls for investing below $54/share, which will become my 2026E PT for the company. That means that the bank has to drop at least 10% for this to be attractive. This is due to both my discounting and the comparatively lower yield.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Burke & Herbert is an interesting regional banking play in a mostly attractive area of the United States, but unfortunately, the bank has been seeing challenging trends both from an operational and integration/merger side. Put into context, I can buy BHRB - or I can buy a 4%+ yielding better banks with a BBB+ credit rating, and a significantly better conservatively-adjusted upside available than this one. In such a situation, my choice is obvious. This investment is, contextually speaking, a "HOLD", even though upside based on its current EPS growth forecast is entirely possible. I simply don't like the risk/reward profile here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $54/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.
